name: Digital Strategy (eBusiness)
exam_format: 1 hour — mix of open and closed-end questions. All three sources required: slides, Digital Human Enterprise textbook, and papers announced in class. Guest speaker sessions may be tested.

sessions.title: SS 1 - Course Introduction
topics: eBusiness vs eCommerce distinction
Digital transformation overview
Information goods economics: high first-copy cost, near-zero marginal cost
Experience goods and the role of DRM
Network effects: direct and indirect
Course structure and assessment

sessions.title: SS 2 - New Landscape and New Players: Towards the Sharing Economy
topics: B2B, B2C, C2C eBusiness models
Platform economy and two-sided markets
Sharing economy: monetising underutilised assets
Revenue models: freemium, subscription, advertising, affiliate
Peer economy and trust mechanisms
Regulatory challenges for platform businesses

sessions.title: SS 3 - The Impact of Digital Transformation on the Value Chain
topics: Physical value chain vs virtual value chain
Decoupling: unbundling of traditionally integrated activities
Recoupling: reassembly in new digital configurations
Infomediaries: aggregating and monetising information flows
New operator types enabled by digital disruption
Intersection of physical and virtual value networks

sessions.title: SS 4 - Bending Spoon (Guest Speaker)
topics: Mobile app economy and growth strategies
Italian tech unicorn: Bending Spoon case
App monetisation models
Growth hacking and user acquisition
Product-led growth in consumer apps
Scaling a digital product company

sessions.title: SS 5 - The Rules of Competition in Digital Settings
topics: Network effects and winner-take-all / winner-take-most dynamics
Multi-homing and its effect on platform moats
Co-opetition: simultaneous competition and cooperation
Chicken-and-egg problems in two-sided markets
Digital ecosystem orchestrators and power asymmetry
Lock-in, switching costs, and competitive moats

sessions.title: SS 6 - New Business Drivers and Entrepreneurial Management
topics: Entrepreneurial capitalism in digital markets
Modular and reconfigurable business models
Speed and agility as competitive advantage
AI and machine learning in eBusiness applications
Big data: volume, velocity, variety
Implications for strategic time horizons

sessions.title: SS 7 - The Role of SEO and SEM in Raising Traffic
topics: SEO: organic ranking through content quality, relevance, and technical optimisation
SEM: paid search advertising and keyword bidding
Google Ads: Quality Score, ad rank, cost per click
Click-through rate (CTR), impressions, cost per acquisition
Search intent and keyword strategy
Google guest session perspective (F. Moro, SDA Bocconi)

slides.title: Google / SEO & SEM Session
slides.file: Digital Strategy/(shared) SDA Bocconi - F. Moro __ Google session (March 2026) (2).pdf

sessions.title: SS 8 - Behavioral Customization
topics: Behavioral targeting: segmenting visitors by real-time on-site actions
Real-time personalisation: delivering differentiated experiences within a session
Fanplayr case: conversion optimisation via behavioral rules
A/B testing: methodology, statistical significance, limitations
Personalisation platform implementation challenges
Data ethics, GDPR, and consumer trust in personalisation

sessions.title: SS 9 - Digital Customer Experience
topics: Customer journey mapping across digital touchpoints
Multichannel vs omnichannel: key distinction and integration requirements
Online-to-offline (O2O) commerce
CRM: tracking interactions, customer profiles, Social CRM
Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) as a CRM metric
Beacon technology, geo-location, and contextual marketing
Personalisation and privacy trade-offs

sessions.title: Wrap Up + Midterm Sample Questions
topics: Midterm exam structure and sample questions
Key themes: value chain, platform models, competitive dynamics
Revision of SS 1–9 core concepts
Exam technique for Bocconi-format MCQs (which is NOT correct?)

sessions.title: SS 10 - Group Assignment Kick Off
topics: Group assignment brief and requirements
Project structure and deliverables
Team formation and topic selection

sessions.title: SS 11 - Quantitative Demand Analysis and ROI Equation
topics: ROI equation for digital marketing investments
Customer Lifetime Value (CLV): formula and drivers
Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) and CAC:CLV ratio
Demand forecasting: time-series analysis, leading indicators
Price elasticity and dynamic pricing in digital markets
Subscription economics: MRR, ARR, churn rate, leaky bucket problem
Digital marketing metrics: CPA, ROAS, CTR vs conversion

sessions.title: SS 12-13 - Web-Enabled and Web-Centric Market Research
topics: Online surveys: reach, cost, and limitations (self-selection bias, satisficing)
Netnography (digital ethnography): observing online communities
Social listening: monitoring brand mentions and sentiment in real time
Web analytics: bounce rate, unique visitors vs sessions, funnel analysis
Zero Moment of Truth (ZMOT): pre-purchase online research
Online consumer behaviour and cognitive biases
Primary vs secondary research in digital contexts
Big data biases and complementarity with traditional research

slides.title: Web-Enabled Market Research
slides.file: Digital Strategy/2026 eBiz ricerche mkt shared.pdf

sessions.title: SS 14 - Costa Guest Session
topics: Digital marketing in the travel and hospitality industry
Costa Cruises: digital transformation case
Customer acquisition and retention in a high-consideration category
Digital touchpoints in a complex customer journey

sessions.title: SS 15 - Social Media Listening
topics: Social listening: systematic monitoring of social platforms for brand signals
Blogmeter / SHIBUMI tools: capabilities and methodology
Sentiment analysis: positive, negative, neutral classification
Key metrics: share of voice, engagement rate, reach, mentions
Crisis detection and reputation management via listening
Translating listening data into actionable marketing insights

sessions.title: SS 16 - Targeting and Strategic Positioning
topics: Digital market segmentation: behavioural, demographic, psychographic
Lookalike audiences: identifying users similar to best customers
Micro-segmentation: precision targeting at lower cost than mass media
GDPR constraints on data-based targeting
Strategic positioning in digital markets
Targeting strategy decisions: reach vs relevance

sessions.title: SS 17-18 - New Product Development and Collaborative Innovation
topics: Open innovation: sourcing ideas beyond firm boundaries
Co-creation with customers and external communities
Crowdsourcing models for product development
Digital platforms enabling collaborative innovation
From closed R&D to open ecosystems
Managing intellectual property in collaborative innovation

sessions.title: SS 19 - Product Development and eCommerce: The Experience from Amazon
topics: Amazon marketplace model: connecting buyers and sellers at scale
Amazon flywheel: how growth reinforces growth
Customer obsession as a product development principle
Working backwards: press release methodology
Amazon's data advantage in product decisions
eCommerce strategy lessons from Amazon

sessions.title: SS 20 - Customer Funnel and eCommerce
topics: Customer funnel: awareness, consideration, purchase, retention, advocacy
Conversion Rate Optimisation (CRO): continuous testing and iteration
Landing page design, CTA placement, page load speed as conversion factors
Mobile commerce (m-commerce) and mobile-first design
Progressive Web Apps (PWAs) vs native apps
eCommerce strategy: cart abandonment, checkout optimisation

sessions.title: SS 21 - Social Media Marketing
topics: Social media platforms: strategic differences and audience profiles
Organic vs paid social: declining organic reach on major platforms
Influencer marketing: types, selection, measurement
User-generated content (UGC) as authentic marketing
Social commerce: integrated shopping within social platforms
Corriere della Sera digital marketing case

slides.title: Corriere Digital Marketing
slides.file: Digital Strategy/Corriere Mktg Digital per Bocconi 2026 selezione.pdf

sessions.title: SS 22 - Content Management
topics: Content marketing: attracting and retaining customers through valuable content
Content as a long-term SEO and brand-building strategy
Content management systems and editorial planning
Thought leadership content in B2B contexts
Measuring content marketing ROI
Content strategy vs paid advertising trade-offs

definitions.definition: Conducting business activities using electronic networks; broader than eCommerce — covers internal operations, supply chain, customer service, and transactions
Subset of eBusiness: specifically the buying and selling of goods/services online. A firm can have eBusiness without eCommerce
Fundamental change in how organisations operate by leveraging digital technologies. Requires changes to business models, culture, and structure — not just IT systems
Unbundling of activities that were traditionally integrated within a single value chain, enabled by digital technologies
Reassembly of decoupled value chain activities in new configurations, enabling new operator types such as infomediaries and pure-play platforms
An intermediary that creates value by collecting, aggregating, and monetising information — enabling better matching between buyers and sellers
Value of a product increases as more users join the same network (e.g. WhatsApp becomes more valuable as more contacts use it)
Growth on one side of a platform increases value for users on the other side (e.g. more Uber drivers → better service for riders)
Strategic dynamic where firms simultaneously compete and cooperate — common in digital platform ecosystems
Users or providers participating in multiple competing platforms simultaneously; weakens any single platform's competitive moat
Revenue model offering basic version free, charging for premium features or additional capacity
Total net profit expected from a customer over the entire relationship; central metric in subscription and platform businesses
Total cost of acquiring one new customer. CAC:CLV ratio is the key indicator of business model sustainability
Integration of all channels so customers experience a seamless, consistent interaction regardless of channel — distinct from multichannel (channels may operate independently)
The online research and evaluation consumers undertake before a purchase decision — coined by Google
Percentage of subscribers who cancel within a period; directly determines sustainability of subscription business economics
Segmenting website visitors based on real-time actions — pages viewed, time on site, purchase intent signals — to deliver personalised offers
Search Engine Optimisation: improving organic (non-paid) search ranking through content quality, relevance, and technical site optimisation. Cannot be purchased directly
Search Engine Marketing: paid search advertising where firms bid on keywords to appear in sponsored results
Sourcing ideas and R&D inputs from outside firm boundaries — customers, partners, communities — rather than relying solely on internal R&D
Digital ethnography: observing and analysing online communities and social media to derive consumer insights without direct interviewing
Systematic monitoring of social platforms for brand mentions, sentiment, competitor activity, and consumer conversations in real time
Ongoing process of testing and improving website/funnel elements to increase the percentage of visitors who complete a desired action. Not a one-time project
Digitisable products with high first-copy costs but near-zero marginal cost of reproduction. Experience goods — quality hard to assess before consumption
Economic model where individuals monetise underutilised assets (cars, homes, skills) through peer-to-peer platforms — e.g. Airbnb, BlaBlaCar

concepts.name: Physical vs Virtual Value Chain
concepts.explanation: The physical value chain handles tangible activities; the virtual value chain runs parallel, managing information flows. Digital transformation enables firms to extract value from both simultaneously. They intersect and interact rather than one replacing the other

concepts.name: Winner-Take-All Dynamics
concepts.explanation: Strong network effects can produce markets dominated by one or few players. But not inevitable — multi-homing, low switching costs, and niche differentiation can sustain competition. Regulatory scrutiny also limits monopoly formation

concepts.name: Chicken-and-Egg Problem
concepts.explanation: Platform must attract two sides simultaneously, but each side waits for the other. Solved by subsidising one side, seeding supply, or targeting a niche where smaller network still has value

concepts.name: Entrepreneurial Capitalism
concepts.explanation: Digital markets reward speed, agility, and continuous innovation over scale. Competitive advantage is more dynamic and time-sensitive. Firms must operate with modular, reconfigurable business configurations

concepts.name: CAC:CLV Ratio
concepts.explanation: Core metric for digital business sustainability. Business only works if CLV > CAC. Subscription businesses need ongoing acquisition just to replace churned customers (leaky bucket). Reducing churn has compounding positive impact on CLV

concepts.name: Big Data Bias
concepts.explanation: Digital behavioural data only captures people who use digital channels — excludes offline populations. Selection bias means online users differ from the general population. Big data complements but does not replace traditional research

concepts.name: Amazon Flywheel
concepts.explanation: Growth reinforces growth: lower prices → more customers → more sellers → more selection → lower costs → lower prices. A self-reinforcing loop that creates compounding competitive advantage

concepts.name: Space-Time Dimensions of Digital Strategy
concepts.explanation: Digital collapses spatial constraints (global reach from day one, cross-industry entry) and temporal constraints (continuous innovation, compressed strategic time horizons). Together these drive 'entrepreneurial capitalism'

distinctions.difference: eCommerce is a subset of eBusiness focused solely on online buying/selling transactions. eBusiness encompasses all digital business activities including internal operations, supply chain, HR, and communication. A firm can have eBusiness without eCommerce

Multichannel = present on multiple channels, but they operate independently with separate data and experiences. Omnichannel = all channels integrated; cart, history, and experience are consistent everywhere. Omnichannel requires significant backend integration investment

SEO = organic ranking earned through quality, relevance, and technical optimisation — cannot be purchased. SEM = paid placement in sponsored results via keyword bidding. High organic ranking is not for sale

Direct: value increases as more users join the same side of the network (e.g. messaging apps). Indirect: growth on one side increases value for the other side (e.g. more app developers → better iPhone for users)

Content marketing builds authority and organic traffic over months/years — poor for immediate results, strong for long-term brand equity. Paid advertising generates traffic within hours but stops when spend stops. Both serve different strategic time horizons

Closed R&D: all innovation sourced internally, proprietary. Open innovation: ideas and inputs sourced from customers, partners, communities, acquisitions. Digital platforms have made open innovation dramatically cheaper and faster

Primary: new data generated directly from target audience (surveys, interviews, usability tests) — bespoke but costly. Secondary: existing data (industry reports, analytics, platform data) — faster and cheaper but may not address the specific question. Good research uses both

examples: Bending Spoon — Italian mobile app unicorn; rapid growth through app store optimisation and acquisition
Fanplayr — behavioural targeting platform; real-time personalisation case for eCommerce conversion
Amazon — flywheel model, customer obsession, marketplace and product development
Airbnb / BlaBlaCar — sharing economy; monetising underutilised assets via peer-to-peer platforms
Blogmeter / SHIBUMI — social listening tools for brand monitoring and sentiment analysis
Corriere della Sera — legacy media organisation navigating digital content and marketing
Costa Cruises — digital marketing in high-consideration travel/hospitality category

questions.question: Regarding the impact of digital technologies on business models, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Digital technologies enabled the emergence of the platform economy and two-sided markets
options.b: The sharing economy created new business models by enabling individuals to monetise underutilised assets
options.c: Infomediaries enable new business models by managing and monetising large amounts of information
options.d: Infomediaries overshadow and replace the role of humans in managing the value chain

questions.explanation: This directly mirrors the sample question from your Digital Strategy exam. Infomediaries augment and transform the value chain — they do not simply replace human roles. The course emphasises that humans remain central; digital tools change how they operate, not whether they are needed.

questions.question: Regarding the decoupling and recoupling of value chains in the digital economy, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Decoupling refers to the unbundling of traditionally integrated activities in the value chain
options.b: Recoupling refers to the reassembly of value chain activities in new configurations enabled by digital technology
options.c: The physical value network and the virtual value network can intersect and interact
options.d: Decoupling and recoupling result in a simpler, more linear value chain with fewer participants

questions.explanation: Decoupling and recoupling create more complex, networked value configurations — not simpler ones. New intermediaries, platform players, and specialist operators emerge. The result is a richer, more diverse value network, not a simplified linear chain.

questions.question: Regarding competitive dynamics in digital markets, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Online competition has shifted from product-based to information-based competition in many industries
options.b: Digital markets have enabled 'cross-industry' competition where firms from other sectors enter traditional markets
options.c: Co-opetition describes a strategy where companies simultaneously compete and cooperate
options.d: In digital markets, competitive advantage is primarily determined by company size, making small firms permanently disadvantaged

questions.explanation: Digital markets can actually level the playing field — small firms with superior data capabilities, network effects, or platform designs can outcompete much larger incumbents. Company size is not the primary determinant of digital competitive advantage.

questions.question: Regarding digital personalisation and customer experience, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Personalisation can apply to content, product, and the overall customer experience
options.b: Beacons and geo-location technology enable contextual marketing tied to physical location
options.c: The omnichannel approach integrates online and offline touchpoints into a seamless customer experience
options.d: Real-time communication between companies and customers is a new capability that existed before digital technologies

questions.explanation: Real-time, scalable, personalised communication between companies and individual customers is a fundamentally new capability enabled by digital technologies. Before digital, mass communication was broadcast (one-to-many) and real-time personalisation at scale was impossible.

questions.question: Regarding the platform economy and two-sided markets, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: A two-sided market connects two distinct user groups who provide each other with network benefits
options.b: Platform businesses can grow rapidly because of network effects — value increases as more users join
options.c: Platforms always charge the same price to both sides of the market
options.d: Examples of platform businesses include marketplaces, social networks, and app stores

questions.explanation: A distinctive feature of two-sided markets is asymmetric pricing. Platforms often charge one side very little or nothing (e.g. users of a social network) while extracting value from the other side (e.g. advertisers). Pricing strategy on each side depends on which group's participation is more valuable to attract.

questions.question: Describe the notion of decoupling and re-coupling of the value chain and explain how this enables the emergence of new types of operators. (4 points)
questions.model_answer: Decoupling refers to the unbundling of activities that were traditionally integrated within a single value chain. Digital technologies allow previously bundled steps — such as information provision, transaction processing, and physical delivery — to be separated and performed by different specialists. Recoupling refers to the reassembly of these activities in new configurations, creating new types of operators who aggregate specific steps across multiple industries or customer journeys. This enables the emergence of infomediaries (information intermediaries who manage data flows), pure-play online retailers, comparison platforms, and marketplace operators who own no physical assets but control customer relationships. The physical and virtual value networks intersect, creating hybrid models. The key insight is that digital technology destroys old value chain logic and enables new entrants to occupy highly profitable niches that were previously inaccessible.
key_points: Give examples of new operator types
Note intersection of physical and virtual networks

questions.question: Illustrate how digital technologies have changed the space-time dimensions of business strategies. (4 points)
questions.model_answer: Digital technologies have collapsed both the spatial and temporal constraints of traditional business strategy. In terms of space: firms can now operate globally from day one, reach any market without physical presence, and compete cross-industry (a technology company can enter financial services, retail, or logistics). Geographic barriers to entry have been dramatically reduced. In terms of time: competition is now characterised by continuous innovation rather than periodic product cycles. Speed to market, real-time customer feedback, and the ability to iterate rapidly have compressed strategic time horizons. This has pushed business towards 'entrepreneurial capitalism' — faster decision-making, modular business configurations that can be reconfigured quickly, and a premium on agility over scale. The combination means that competitive advantage is more dynamic, contested, and time-sensitive than in pre-digital markets.
key_points: Address both space (geographic) and time (speed) dimensions
Continuous innovation / entrepreneurial capitalism as temporal change

questions.question: Explain what is meant by co-opetition in digital markets and why it has become more prevalent with digital transformation.
questions.model_answer: Co-opetition describes a strategic dynamic where firms simultaneously compete and cooperate. In digital markets this is common because: (1) platform ecosystems create situations where firms need each other's participation even as they compete for value share; (2) open standards and APIs create interdependencies; (3) the speed of innovation makes it rational to partner in some areas while competing in others. For example, Google and Apple compete in smartphones but cooperate on standards. Firms cooperate on technology infrastructure and compete on customer experience. Co-opetition is more prevalent in digital markets because the modular, networked nature of digital business creates more opportunities for partial overlap — unlike traditional industries where competition was more binary.
key_points: Explain why it arises (network effects, platform ecosystems)

questions.question: Regarding the distinction between eBusiness and eCommerce, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: eCommerce refers specifically to online buying and selling transactions between parties
options.b: eBusiness encompasses a broader set of activities including internal operations, supply chain management, and customer service
options.c: An organisation can engage in eBusiness activities without necessarily conducting eCommerce transactions
options.d: eBusiness and eCommerce are interchangeable terms that refer to identical concepts

questions.explanation: eBusiness is the broader concept — it covers all aspects of conducting business electronically (internal processes, supply chain, HR, communication, collaboration). eCommerce is a subset that refers specifically to buying and selling transactions online. A firm can digitise its internal operations (eBusiness) without running an online store (eCommerce).

questions.question: Regarding digital transformation in organisations, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Digital transformation involves changes to business models, processes, and customer value propositions
options.b: Digital transformation is solely a technology project and does not require changes to organisational culture or structure
options.c: Incumbent firms face organisational inertia challenges when undergoing digital transformation
options.d: Digital transformation can create new revenue streams by enabling entirely new business models

questions.explanation: Digital transformation is not a technology project alone. It requires fundamental changes to organisational culture, structure, capabilities, and mindset — not just IT systems. Firms that treat it as purely technical typically fail to capture the value. Cultural and organisational change is usually the harder and more critical dimension.

questions.question: Regarding the economics of information goods (digitisable products), which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Information goods have high first-copy costs but near-zero marginal cost of reproduction and distribution
options.b: Information goods are experience goods — consumers often cannot fully assess quality before consumption
options.c: Because digital goods can be copied at zero cost, intellectual property mechanisms such as DRM have no role in digital markets
options.d: Versioning — selling differentiated versions of the same information good at different price points — is a key pricing strategy in digital markets

questions.explanation: The near-zero reproduction cost of digital goods makes intellectual property protection more important, not irrelevant. Without DRM, licensing, or legal frameworks, firms cannot recoup the high fixed costs of creating information goods. The economics of information goods require firms to find ways to prevent unlimited free copying — hence DRM, paywalls, and licensing arrangements remain central to digital content business models.

questions.question: Regarding network effects in digital markets, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Direct network effects occur when the value of a product increases as more users join the same network
options.b: Indirect network effects occur when growth on one side of a platform increases value for users on the other side
options.c: Network effects guarantee that the firm with the largest network will always remain dominant regardless of competitor innovations
options.d: Platform businesses can leverage network effects to achieve rapid growth and create significant barriers to entry

questions.explanation: Network effects confer advantages but do not guarantee permanent dominance. Competitors can overcome them through superior technology, better user experience, disruptive business models, or by targeting unserved segments. Multi-homing (users joining multiple platforms) also weakens the protective power of network effects. MySpace had strong network effects but was displaced by Facebook; Skype by WhatsApp.

questions.question: Regarding the competitive dynamics created by digital transformation, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Digital technologies have enabled cross-industry competition, with firms from outside traditional industry boundaries entering new markets
options.b: The 'entrepreneurial capitalism' concept emphasises that digital markets favour speed, agility, and continuous innovation
options.c: Digital markets always result in perfect competition because information is freely available and switching costs are eliminated
options.d: Digital transformation has compressed strategic time horizons, requiring firms to innovate more continuously than in pre-digital eras

questions.explanation: Digital markets do not produce perfect competition — they often produce the opposite. Network effects, data advantages, switching costs, ecosystem lock-in, and platform control create strong competitive moats. Many digital markets tend toward oligopoly or monopoly. The course's emphasis on winner-take-all dynamics and platform power directly contradicts the perfect competition claim.

questions.question: Regarding the components of a digital business model, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: A business model describes how a company creates, delivers, and captures value
options.b: The revenue model is a key component of the business model, defining how the firm generates income
options.c: Digital business models always require the firm to own the physical assets involved in value delivery
options.d: The value proposition defines the benefits a firm offers customers and the problems it solves for them

questions.explanation: Asset-light models are a defining innovation of digital business. Airbnb owns no hotels; Uber owns no cars; Spotify owns no recording studios. Digital platforms create value by orchestrating assets owned by third parties. Ownership of physical assets is neither required nor always desirable in digital business models — it is the marketplace or platform that controls the customer relationship and data.

questions.question: Regarding differences between B2B and B2C eBusiness models, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: B2B transactions typically involve longer sales cycles and more complex decision-making processes than B2C
options.b: B2B eCommerce markets are larger in aggregate transaction volume than B2C markets globally
options.c: In B2B markets, purchasing decisions are always made by a single individual, unlike B2C where group decisions are more common
options.d: B2B digital platforms often include features like personalised pricing, dedicated catalogues, and procurement system integration

questions.explanation: The reality is the reverse. B2B purchasing typically involves buying centres — multiple stakeholders including users, technical evaluators, financial decision-makers, and executives. This is why B2B sales cycles are longer and more complex. In B2C, while household decisions can involve multiple people, individual purchasing decisions are far more common than in B2B.

questions.question: Regarding digital business revenue models, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The freemium model offers a basic version free of charge and charges for premium features or additional capacity
options.b: A subscription model generates recurring revenue by charging customers on a periodic basis
options.c: Advertising-based revenue models require the firm to charge users directly for access to content or services
options.d: Affiliate marketing generates revenue by earning commissions when referred customers complete purchases on partner sites

questions.explanation: Advertising-based models are funded by advertisers, not users — users typically receive access for free. This is the 'if you're not paying, you're the product' dynamic. Google Search, Facebook, and most news sites use advertising models precisely because they do not charge users, instead monetising user attention and data. Charging users directly is the opposite of an advertising model.

questions.question: Regarding the virtual value chain and infomediaries, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The virtual value chain runs parallel to the physical value chain and involves the gathering, processing, and distribution of information
options.b: Infomediaries create value by collecting, aggregating, and monetising information that enables better matching between buyers and sellers
options.c: The virtual value chain has entirely replaced the physical value chain in most industries
options.d: Companies that master both the physical and virtual value chains can achieve distinctive competitive positions

questions.explanation: The virtual and physical value chains coexist and intersect — one has not replaced the other in most industries. Even heavily digitalised sectors like retail, financial services, or logistics rely on physical infrastructure. The course explicitly discusses the intersection of physical and virtual networks as a strategic opportunity, not a transition where the physical disappears.

questions.question: Regarding C2C eBusiness models and the sharing economy, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: C2C platforms like eBay or Vinted enable individuals to sell to other individuals, with the platform earning fees or advertising revenue
options.b: The sharing economy creates value by enabling individuals to monetise underutilised assets such as cars, homes, or skills
options.c: C2C and peer economy models eliminate all need for trust mechanisms since all participants are non-commercial individuals
options.d: Regulatory uncertainty is a significant challenge for peer economy platforms as they often blur traditional industry category boundaries

questions.explanation: Trust is critical — and harder to establish — in C2C contexts precisely because participants are private individuals rather than established commercial entities. Platforms invest heavily in reputation systems (star ratings, reviews), identity verification, payment escrow, and insurance precisely to solve this trust problem. Airbnb's host guarantee and eBay's feedback system are direct responses to the trust challenge in peer transactions.

questions.question: Regarding the digital customer journey, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The customer journey maps all touchpoints a customer has with a brand from initial awareness through to post-purchase engagement
options.b: Customer journey mapping helps firms identify pain points and opportunities to improve the customer experience
options.c: In digital markets, the customer journey is strictly linear and always follows the same sequence of awareness, consideration, and purchase
options.d: Post-purchase experience and loyalty phases are increasingly important parts of the digital customer journey

questions.explanation: Digital customer journeys are non-linear. Consumers switch between devices and channels, re-enter the journey at different stages, share experiences on social media that influence others, and make purchases outside the 'expected' sequence. A customer might discover a product on Instagram, research via Google, watch a YouTube review, purchase via mobile, and seek support on Twitter. Journey mapping must account for this complexity.

questions.question: Regarding omnichannel and multichannel strategies, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: A multichannel strategy involves a brand being present on multiple channels, but those channels may operate independently with separate data and experiences
options.b: An omnichannel strategy integrates all channels so customers experience a seamless, consistent interaction regardless of which channel they use
options.c: Omnichannel and multichannel strategies are interchangeable terms that describe the same approach to customer engagement
options.d: Online-to-offline (O2O) commerce connects digital interactions with physical store experiences and is a dimension of omnichannel strategy

questions.explanation: Omnichannel and multichannel are distinct. A multichannel firm may have a website, app, and stores that each operate independently — different inventory, different pricing, different data silos. An omnichannel firm integrates all these so the customer's cart, history, and experience is consistent everywhere. The distinction matters strategically: omnichannel requires significant backend integration investment.

questions.question: Regarding digital personalisation and customer data, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Data collected across digital touchpoints can enable personalised product recommendations and targeted communications
options.b: Beacon technology enables location-based marketing by communicating with customers' mobile devices near a physical store
options.c: Personalisation based on individual customer data always increases customer satisfaction with no privacy or ethical implications
options.d: Contextual marketing delivers relevant content or offers based on the real-time context of the customer, such as location or time of day

questions.explanation: Personalisation at scale using individual data raises significant privacy, consent, and ethical concerns. GDPR and consumer expectations around data use mean that personalisation can feel intrusive or manipulative if done without transparency. The course — and the Fanplayr case — acknowledges that personalisation effectiveness must be balanced against trust and regulatory compliance.

questions.question: Regarding Customer Relationship Management (CRM) in digital business, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Digital CRM systems enable firms to track customer interactions across multiple channels and touchpoints
options.b: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) is a key CRM metric representing the total net profit expected from a customer over the entire relationship
options.c: CRM systems are only useful for large companies with millions of customers and offer no benefit to small businesses
options.d: Social CRM extends traditional CRM to incorporate social media data, enabling more complete customer profiles and social engagement tracking

questions.explanation: CRM tools are valuable at all scales. For small businesses, CRM systems provide structure for managing customer relationships, tracking interactions, and identifying high-value customers — functions that become harder to manage manually as a customer base grows beyond a handful of contacts. Cloud-based CRM tools (HubSpot, Pipedrive) are specifically designed for and widely adopted by small businesses.

questions.question: Regarding search engine marketing (SEM) and search engine optimisation (SEO), which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: SEO involves optimising website content and structure to improve organic (non-paid) ranking in search engine results
options.b: SEM typically refers to paid search advertising, where firms bid on keywords to appear in sponsored results
options.c: A high organic search ranking can be purchased directly from the search engine for a fixed monthly fee
options.d: Click-through rate (CTR) is a key metric in search marketing, measuring the proportion of users who click on a given result

questions.explanation: Organic search rankings cannot be purchased — that is the fundamental distinction between SEO (earned through quality, relevance, and technical optimisation) and paid search (SEM). Selling guaranteed organic rankings would undermine the integrity of search results, which is why search engines do not offer this. What can be purchased is placement in the sponsored/ads section, not organic rankings.

questions.question: Regarding social media marketing, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Social media enables two-way dialogue between brands and consumers, unlike traditional broadcast advertising
options.b: Influencer marketing involves partnering with individuals who have social media followings to promote products or services
options.c: Organic social media reach has generally increased over time on major platforms as those platforms have grown larger
options.d: User-generated content serves as authentic marketing material and can build consumer trust more effectively than brand-produced content

questions.explanation: Organic reach has declined significantly on major platforms like Facebook and Instagram. As platforms have grown, they have prioritised content from friends and paid advertising over brand pages, reducing the percentage of followers who see a brand's organic posts. This is partly a business model choice — declining organic reach pushes brands toward paid promotion. This trend is one of the key challenges in social media marketing strategy.

questions.question: Regarding email marketing and digital direct marketing, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Email marketing offers high return on investment relative to many other digital channels because of its low cost per message
options.b: Segmentation and personalisation in email campaigns can significantly improve open rates and conversion rates
options.c: Under GDPR and similar regulations, companies can send marketing emails to any address they can find without prior consent
options.d: A/B testing of subject lines, content, and send times is standard practice in professional email marketing

questions.explanation: GDPR (and similar laws such as CAN-SPAM and CASL) requires that recipients have explicitly consented to receive marketing communications. Sending unsolicited marketing emails is illegal under GDPR and can result in significant fines. Legitimate email marketing requires a permission-based list, clear opt-in mechanisms, and easy unsubscribe options. This is a fundamental legal and ethical constraint on email marketing.

questions.question: Regarding digital marketing metrics and measurement, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Cost per acquisition (CPA) measures how much it costs to acquire one customer through a specific marketing channel
options.b: Return on advertising spend (ROAS) measures revenue generated for every unit of currency spent on advertising
options.c: A high click-through rate (CTR) always indicates that a campaign is profitable and generating positive return on investment
options.d: Attribution modelling addresses the challenge of assigning credit for a conversion to the correct touchpoint in a multi-channel customer journey

questions.explanation: CTR measures engagement with an ad but says nothing about what happens after the click. A campaign could have a high CTR but generate no purchases (poor landing page, irrelevant audience, wrong product-market fit). ROI depends on what happens downstream: conversion rate, average order value, and margins. A high CTR with poor conversion is worse than a lower CTR with strong conversion.

questions.question: Regarding content marketing in digital business, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Content marketing aims to attract and retain customers by creating and distributing valuable, relevant content
options.b: Content marketing can improve SEO performance by generating high-quality pages and inbound links
options.c: Content marketing delivers immediate, guaranteed results and consistently outperforms paid advertising for short-term lead generation
options.d: Thought leadership content — white papers, webinars, expert articles — is particularly effective in B2B content marketing

questions.explanation: Content marketing is a long-term strategy. It builds authority, organic traffic, and audience trust over time — typically months or years — before generating significant ROI. It is generally poor at delivering immediate results compared to paid advertising, which can generate traffic and leads within hours. The comparison between content marketing (brand building, long-term) and paid advertising (immediate, scalable but costly) is a classic strategic tradeoff.

questions.question: Regarding platform economics and winner-take-all dynamics in digital markets, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Strong network effects can lead to winner-take-all or winner-take-most outcomes in platform markets
options.b: Multi-homing — where users or providers participate in multiple competing platforms — weakens a platform's competitive moat
options.c: All digital platform markets inevitably converge on a single dominant monopolist, making competition policy irrelevant
options.d: Chicken-and-egg problems arise when a platform must attract two sides of the market simultaneously and each side waits for the other

questions.explanation: Many digital platform markets sustain multiple competing platforms, especially where multi-homing is easy. Dating apps, food delivery, ride-hailing, and B2B marketplaces often have several viable competitors. Winner-take-all is more likely when switching costs are very high, multi-homing is costly, and network effects are very strong — but it is not inevitable. Competition policy remains highly relevant in platform markets precisely because of the risk (not certainty) of monopoly.

questions.question: Regarding the role of artificial intelligence in eBusiness, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: AI enables more sophisticated personalisation by processing large volumes of behavioural data to predict customer preferences
options.b: Chatbots powered by AI can handle routine customer service inquiries at scale without human intervention
options.c: AI-driven recommendation systems have no significant impact on consumer purchasing behaviour or platform revenues
options.d: Machine learning algorithms require substantial volumes of training data to generate reliable predictions

questions.explanation: AI recommendation systems drive substantial revenue. Amazon attributes approximately 35% of its revenue to recommendation algorithms. Netflix estimates its recommendation engine saves over $1 billion annually in customer retention. These systems are among the highest-ROI applications of AI in digital commerce. The idea that recommendations have no significant impact is directly contradicted by extensive industry evidence.

questions.question: Regarding big data and its application in digital market research, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Big data is characterised by volume, velocity, and variety — the three Vs describing its scale, speed, and diversity of types
options.b: Big data analytics can reveal customer behavioural patterns that would be invisible through traditional small-sample survey research
options.c: Because big data is derived from real customer actions, it is always unbiased and superior to all other forms of market research
options.d: Data governance and quality management are critical challenges when implementing big data analytics capabilities

questions.explanation: Big data has its own inherent biases. Digital behavioural data only captures people who use digital channels — excluding offline populations. Selection bias means the sample of people who engage online differs from the general population. Data collected passively can also reflect existing inequalities or platform design choices. Big data is a powerful complement to, not a replacement for, other research methods — and its quality and biases must be critically evaluated.

questions.question: Regarding mobile commerce (m-commerce) and mobile consumer experience, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Mobile devices have become a primary channel for product research, price comparison, and purchase in many consumer markets
options.b: Mobile-first design prioritises the user experience on smaller screens, then adapts for desktop
options.c: Progressive Web Apps (PWAs) require users to download and install them from app stores, making access harder than native apps
options.d: Push notifications are a key mobile app feature enabling brands to re-engage users with timely, personalised messages

questions.explanation: PWAs are specifically designed to avoid the app store download requirement. They run in the browser, can be 'added to homescreen' without an app store visit, load quickly, and work offline via service workers. The ability to bypass app store distribution (and its associated costs and approval processes) is one of the key advantages of PWAs over native apps — not a disadvantage.

questions.question: Regarding digital ecosystems and their strategic implications, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: A digital ecosystem is a network of organisations, individuals, and technologies that interact and co-evolve around a digital platform
options.b: Ecosystem orchestrators — firms controlling key platform infrastructure or data — typically capture disproportionate value from the ecosystem
options.c: All participants in a digital ecosystem have equal power and capture equal value relative to the ecosystem orchestrator
options.d: Lock-in effects and switching costs are mechanisms through which ecosystem orchestrators maintain platform attractiveness and retain participants

questions.explanation: Power and value distribution within ecosystems is deeply asymmetric. Orchestrators (Apple App Store, Amazon Marketplace, Google Play) set the rules, take a percentage of all transactions, and control access to users — while app developers and sellers are dependent on the platform. Many ecosystem participants accept unfavourable terms because the alternative (leaving the ecosystem) means losing access to the user base. This asymmetry is a defining feature of platform power.

questions.question: Regarding online market research methods, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Online surveys allow firms to reach large samples quickly and at lower cost than face-to-face interviews
options.b: Netnography (digital ethnography) involves observing and analysing online communities and social media to derive consumer insights
options.c: Online market research always produces more accurate results than offline methods because respondents are anonymous and face no social desirability bias
options.d: Social listening tools can monitor brand mentions, sentiment, and consumer conversations across social platforms in real time

questions.explanation: Online research has its own biases and limitations. Self-selection means only certain people complete online surveys. Samples may not represent the general population. Online respondents can still face social desirability effects (trying to appear rational, responsible, or fashionable) and are susceptible to satisficing (giving quick, low-effort answers). Additionally, survey fraud and bot responses are practical issues. Online and offline methods have complementary strengths.

questions.question: Regarding online consumer behaviour, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: The zero moment of truth (ZMOT) describes the online research and evaluation that consumers undertake before a purchase decision
options.b: Online reviews and ratings significantly influence consumer purchasing decisions, especially where product quality is hard to assess in advance
options.c: Online consumers are purely rational decision-makers who always search exhaustively and invariably choose the lowest-priced option
options.d: Digital comparison tools have reduced information asymmetry in many markets by making price and product information more transparent

questions.explanation: Behavioural economics research consistently shows that online consumers are subject to cognitive biases — anchoring on first prices seen, preferring default options, being influenced by framing, reviews, and recommendations, and responding to scarcity cues. Convenience, trust, brand familiarity, and interface design often matter more than price. The 'rational economic man' model does not describe real online consumer behaviour.

questions.question: Regarding web analytics and digital performance measurement, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Bounce rate measures the percentage of visitors who leave a website after viewing only one page without further interaction
options.b: Unique visitors and sessions are different metrics: unique visitors counts distinct individuals; sessions counts visits including repeat visits
options.c: Web analytics tools provide complete, perfect data on all users, including those who use ad blockers or have disabled cookies
options.d: Funnel analysis maps how users move through a defined sequence of steps toward a conversion goal, identifying where drop-offs occur

questions.explanation: Web analytics data is necessarily incomplete. Ad blockers prevent tracking scripts from loading. Browser privacy features (Safari ITP, Firefox Enhanced Tracking Protection) limit cookie duration. Users who opt out of tracking or use private browsing are invisible to standard analytics. GDPR consent requirements mean many users decline tracking. Real analytics professionals apply statistical corrections for known data gaps — treating raw analytics numbers as perfect is a common error.

questions.question: Regarding digital market segmentation and targeting, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Behavioural segmentation in digital markets uses data on purchase history, browsing behaviour, and engagement patterns
options.b: Lookalike audiences are created by identifying users who share characteristics with a firm's best existing customers
options.c: In digital markets, it is possible and legally unrestricted to use any customer data for targeting without limitation
options.d: Micro-segmentation enables firms to target very specific consumer groups with tailored messages at lower cost than traditional mass media

questions.explanation: Data use for targeting is heavily regulated. GDPR restricts the collection and processing of personal data of EU citizens, requiring lawful basis (typically consent or legitimate interest), data minimisation, and purpose limitation. Sensitive categories (health, political views) face even stricter rules. Third-party data brokerage and cross-site tracking are under increasing regulatory and platform-level restrictions. The idea of unrestricted data use for targeting is precisely what regulators are challenging.

questions.question: Regarding primary and secondary research in digital market analysis, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Secondary research analyses existing data — such as industry reports, government statistics, or platform analytics
options.b: Primary research generates new data directly from the target audience through surveys, interviews, or usability tests
options.c: Secondary research is always preferable to primary research and the two methods address identical research questions
options.d: Digital tools have blurred the line between secondary and primary research by making real-time behavioural data available as an alternative to self-reported survey data

questions.explanation: Secondary and primary research serve different purposes and neither is universally preferable. Secondary research is faster and cheaper but may not address the specific question, may be outdated, or may lack the granularity required. Primary research answers bespoke questions with fresh data but costs more time and money. Good research design uses both in complementary ways — secondary to build context and hypothesis, primary to test and fill gaps.

questions.question: Regarding demand forecasting in digital markets, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Time-series analysis uses historical sales data to identify patterns and project future demand
options.b: Digital platforms have access to large volumes of real-time data that can improve the accuracy and timeliness of demand forecasts
options.c: A demand forecast produced by a digital algorithm is always accurate because data eliminates all uncertainty
options.d: Leading indicators — such as search volume trends, social media activity, or website traffic — can serve as early signals of demand change

questions.explanation: All forecasts carry uncertainty — including algorithmic ones. Digital data improves signal quality and timeliness but cannot eliminate uncertainty arising from novel events, behavioural shifts, competitive moves, or macroeconomic shocks. Amazon, with arguably the world's best demand forecasting capability, still experiences stockouts and overstock situations. Forecasting is fundamentally probabilistic, and the value of algorithms is in reducing — not eliminating — forecast error.

questions.question: Regarding price sensitivity and elasticity in digital markets, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Price comparison websites and search engines have increased price transparency in many consumer markets
options.b: Dynamic pricing — adjusting prices in real time based on demand, competition, and customer characteristics — is widely used by digital businesses
options.c: In digital markets, lower prices always lead to higher profitability because marginal cost of serving additional customers is near zero
options.d: Personalised pricing based on willingness-to-pay data raises ethical questions and faces increasing regulatory scrutiny

questions.explanation: Even with near-zero marginal cost of digital delivery, lower prices reduce revenue per unit and may not be compensated by sufficient volume increase if demand is inelastic. Customer acquisition also has cost (marketing, sales, onboarding) regardless of marginal delivery cost. The near-zero marginal cost argument applies to delivery, not to the full economics of the business. Pricing decisions must consider contribution margin, positioning, and competitive dynamics — not just marginal cost.

questions.question: Regarding Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) in digital business, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: CLV is calculated based on expected future revenue from a customer, adjusted for retention probability and cost to serve
options.b: In subscription businesses, reducing churn rate has a significant positive compounding impact on CLV
options.c: CLV is only relevant for companies with physical products and has no application in digital or service businesses
options.d: The ratio of Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) to CLV is a key indicator of business model sustainability

questions.explanation: CLV is especially important for digital and subscription businesses, where recurring revenue and long-term customer retention are the central value-creation mechanisms. Netflix, Spotify, SaaS companies, and digital marketplaces are more dependent on CLV thinking than most physical product companies. The entire economics of subscription business — and the decision about how much to spend acquiring a customer — hinges on CLV calculations.

questions.question: Regarding demand drivers in the digital economy, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Network effects — where value increases as more users adopt a product — are a demand-side driver particular to digital and platform markets
options.b: Switching costs created by data lock-in or integration dependencies can reduce price sensitivity and sustain customer relationships
options.c: In digital markets, demand is driven purely by product features and price, making brand equity and consumer trust irrelevant
options.d: Complementary products and services (such as apps for a smartphone platform) can drive demand for the primary platform

questions.explanation: Brand equity and trust are significant demand drivers in digital markets, particularly in categories involving personal data, financial transactions, health, or privacy. Consumers are more likely to share data, make purchases, or adopt a platform from a brand they trust. Trust asymmetry is one of the reasons incumbent platforms retain users even when competitors offer superior features — and why new entrants investing heavily in trust-building often outperform those that do not.

questions.question: Regarding subscription business models in digital markets, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR) and Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR) are key financial metrics for subscription businesses
options.b: Churn rate — the percentage of subscribers who cancel within a period — directly determines the sustainability of subscription business economics
options.c: Subscription models eliminate the need to invest in customer acquisition because subscribers are permanently committed and never leave
options.d: Subscription businesses benefit from more predictable revenue streams than transactional businesses, aiding financial planning and investor valuation

questions.explanation: Churn is inherent to subscription businesses — subscribers leave due to price sensitivity, lifecycle changes, competition, or dissatisfaction. Subscriber attrition means continuous acquisition investment is required just to maintain revenue (the 'leaky bucket' problem). Growing a subscription business requires acquisition well in excess of churn. This is why CAC:CLV ratio is so central to subscription economics: the business only works if the lifetime value of customers acquired exceeds the cost of acquiring and retaining them.

questions.question: Regarding behavioural targeting and real-time personalisation (as illustrated in the Fanplayr case), which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Behavioural targeting segments website visitors based on real-time actions such as pages viewed, time on site, and purchase intent signals
options.b: Real-time personalisation can deliver different offers, messages, or experiences to visitors based on their predicted behaviour or segment
options.c: Behavioural targeting can operate within the same browsing session, influencing the user experience in real time
options.d: Behavioural targeting guarantees conversion for every visitor shown a personalised offer, provided the offer is sufficiently attractive

questions.explanation: Targeting improves conversion rates at a population level — it does not guarantee conversion for any individual visitor. Many visitors will not convert regardless of the offer, due to non-intent browsing, budget constraints, or simply not being at the right point in the purchase journey. The value of behavioural targeting is statistical: shifting the conversion rate from, say, 2% to 3% across millions of sessions creates substantial revenue — but individual conversion is never guaranteed.

questions.question: Regarding conversion rate optimisation (CRO) in digital business, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Conversion rate is calculated as the number of conversions divided by the total number of visitors or sessions
options.b: Small improvements in conversion rate can generate significant revenue impact, especially on high-traffic websites
options.c: CRO is a one-time project that, once completed, requires no further testing or iteration
options.d: Landing page design, call-to-action placement, and page load speed are among the factors that can influence conversion rates

questions.explanation: CRO is a continuous process, not a one-time fix. User behaviour evolves, competitive context changes, seasonal patterns shift, and new product ranges change what is optimal. What works today may be outperformed by a new variant tomorrow. Professional CRO programmes maintain ongoing A/B testing and experimentation roadmaps, continuously seeking incremental improvements. The idea of a finished, permanent optimal state is inconsistent with how digital markets and user behaviour actually work.

questions.question: Regarding A/B testing in digital marketing and product optimisation, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: A/B testing compares two or more variants of a webpage, email, or ad to determine which performs better on a defined metric
options.b: Statistical significance is required before drawing conclusions from A/B tests to ensure results are not due to random chance
options.c: A/B test results from one audience or time period can always be directly applied to different audiences or future periods without further validation
options.d: Multivariate testing extends A/B testing to simultaneously test multiple variables and their interactions

questions.explanation: A/B test results have limited external validity. A test run on UK visitors in Q4 (peak shopping season) may not predict results for different audiences, geographies, or times of year. User segments respond differently to the same changes. This is why CRO programmes run ongoing tests rather than applying a single historical result indefinitely. Novelty effects (initial engagement with anything new) can also cause A/B results to fade over time.

questions.question: Regarding the implementation of personalisation platforms in digital businesses, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Integrating a personalisation platform with existing CRM, analytics, and eCommerce systems is typically a significant technical challenge
options.b: High-quality, clean data is a prerequisite for effective personalisation — poor data quality leads to poor targeting outcomes
options.c: Once a personalisation platform is implemented, it operates autonomously and requires no ongoing management or optimisation
options.d: Change management — ensuring marketing and commercial teams adopt data-driven approaches — is a key implementation challenge

questions.explanation: Personalisation platforms require continuous management: models need retraining as customer behaviour evolves, audience segments need updating, rules require reviewing for relevance, and new use cases need designing and testing. The technical implementation is often the easier part; sustained business value requires ongoing human oversight, strategic decision-making, and iterative optimisation. This is consistent with the Fanplayr case emphasis on partnership and ongoing collaboration, not a 'set-and-forget' deployment.

questions.question: Regarding data ethics and consumer trust in digital marketing, which of the following statements is NOT correct?

options.a: Transparency about data collection and usage practices can increase consumer trust and willingness to share personal information
options.b: GDPR imposes obligations on firms processing personal data of EU citizens, including requirements for consent and data minimisation
options.c: Consumer trust in data usage is irrelevant for digital businesses because most consumers are indifferent to how their data is used
options.d: First-party data — collected directly from customers through owned channels — is increasingly valuable as third-party cookie restrictions tighten

questions.explanation: Consumer awareness of and concern about data privacy has grown substantially. Post-Cambridge Analytica, post-GDPR, and with continued media coverage of data breaches, consumers increasingly factor data practices into brand trust and purchasing decisions. Research consistently shows significant segments of consumers who actively choose providers based on privacy practices. For digital businesses, trust is a competitive differentiator — and its erosion (through a breach or scandal) can cause immediate and lasting revenue damage.
